title: What UAE’s OPEC departure means for gas prices | CNN Business
text:
Source: CNN

The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries, OPEC, is about to lose one of its biggest members. The departure of the United Arab Emirates will deliver a blow to the cartel that could jeopardize its ability to influence the global oil market.

The UAE is OPEC’s third biggest crude oil producer after Saudi Arabia and Iraq. Its exit from the group will allow it to pump more oil,which could help bring oil and gas prices down in the long-run.

But if you’re expecting any immediate relief from high pump prices, you may be disappointed.

Brent crude, the global oil benchmark, is currently trading at multi-week highs of around $117 a barrel.And the national average US gas price is at afour-year highof around $4.23.

But analysts say the impact on oil prices of the UAE’s move will be limited for as long as the Strait of Hormuz remains largely shut, currently choking off some 10-12 million barrels of crude a day from global markets.

The UAE has been “itching to pump more oil,” having invested heavily in expanding its production infrastructure in recent years, according to David Oxley, chief climate and commodities economist at Capital Economics.

OPEChas constrained the UAE’s ability to pump freely because the group imposes production quotas on its members to regulate supply and demand. Critics accuse the group of manipulating prices, something it denies.

The most recent OPEC quotas limited the UAE’s oil output to 3.2 million barrels a day, when it in facthas capacity to produce closer to 5 million barrels a day, Robin Mills, the CEO of Dubai-based consultancy QamarEnergy, told CNN’s Connect the World.

The potential additional supply would meet around 1-2% of daily global oil demand.

OPEC was formed in 1960 by five of the world’s biggest oil producers at the time, namely, Saudi Arabia, Iran, Iraq, Kuwait and Venezuela. The group expanded to 16 member countries at its peak, but currently has 12 members, after Ecuador, Indonesia, Qatar and Angola exited in recent years.

To be sure, the cartel is less powerful than it once was. When OPEC members halted exports to the United States and other countries during the infamous Arab oil embargo, amid the Arab-Israeli conflictin 1973, global oil prices soared 300% and tipped most Western economies into recession.

OPEC’s influence has waned in part since then because the oil-guzzling United States is now anet oil exporter(although it still imports some types of crude oil and petroleum products). The global economy has also become a lot less oil intensive thanks to electrification, energy efficiency and natural gas and renewables taking on a bigger share of power generation.

To shore up its authority, the group expanded in 2016 to bring other oil-producers, such as Russia, under the OPEC+ umbrella.

OPEC+ accounts for nearly 42% of global crude oil production, according to the group’s own figures, so its decisions over oil output can still influence prices.

Since global crude oil prices are determined by supply and demand, in theory, once the Strait of Hormuz reopens, higher supply should bring prices down. (Demand for crude oil is generally driven by the strength of economic growth, which lifts or lowers the purchases of refined oil products such as gasoline and diesel.)

Crude prices could also move lower if the UAE’s exit leads other OPEC members to abandon the group, or if a price-war ensues once the Iran war is over as Gulf producers, including Saudi Arabia, rush to claim market share.

The UAE’s shock departure from OPEC may be timed to secure a larger slice of the global oil market at a time when many countries are scrambling for supply, said Michael Tamvakis, a commodities professor at Bayes Business School in London.

“Once the Straits reopen, the UAE will ramp up supply quickly to replenish the dried-up supply chain and do so without OPEC’s bureaucratic quota negotiations every few months,” he noted.

Before the war, the world was facing an oil supply glut as growth in global oil production, led by the Americas, outpaced demand. OPEC had also started to unwind some of its quotas, leading to increased production. The International Energy Agencysaidback in 2024 that the surplus could “upend” OPEC’s control of the market.

To underline the point: Brent crude was trading around $60 a barrel at the start of the year and around $73 immediately before the United States and Israel attacked Iran on February 28. WTI, the US benchmark, was around $67 just before the war. It is now hovering around $105 a barrel.

The UAE’s decision to quit OPEC will likely add to pressure on crude oil prices over time, feeding into lower prices at the pump.

“A more fractious and weaker OPEC could constrain the group’s influence on oil prices,” economists at Capital Economics, Hamad Hussein and Jason Tuvey, wrote in a note. “That could… skew the balance of risks toward lower oil prices over time.”
